metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each AC, add a concrete proving command: e.g. AC1 → `pytest tests/test_verify_authorship.py -k 'merge_gate'` plus `rebar verify-authorship 2>&1; echo $?` (expect 1 with config ON); AC3 → `rebar verify-authorship --all` after compaction (expect exit 0).
findings.finding: None of the three acceptance criteria provides a concrete proving command (e.g. a `pytest -k ...` invocation or `rebar verify-authorship` CLI call with expected exit code/output), so there is no executable evidence of done-ness beyond 'a test asserts'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit mapping from `registry.Verdict` fields (`verified: bool`, `verdict: str` values: "certified", "mismatch", "unknown_principal", "unavailable", etc.) to the plan's four gate-level labels (verified/unsigned/unknown-author/bad-signature), co-located with the merge-gate classification logic in Section 4.
findings.finding: The plan defines four cryptographic verdict strings for the merge-gate output (verified, unsigned, unknown-author, bad-signature) that conflict with the actual `registry.Verdict.verdict` vocabulary emitted by the existing codebase ("certified", "mismatch", "unknown_principal", "unavailable", "invalid", "unknown_kind", "unknown_scheme"). These vocabularies are never reconciled, so an implementer writing the classify logic in verify_authorship.py will not know which `Verdict.verdict` values map to which plan-level label.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a classification table to Section 4: `verified` ← `Verdict.verified is True and verdict=='certified'`; `unknown-author` ← `Verdict.verdict=='unknown_principal'`; `bad-signature` ← `Verdict.verdict in ('mismatch','unavailable','invalid','unknown_kind','unknown_scheme')`; `unsigned` ← `author_sig` field absent on the event envelope (checked before calling verify_authorship_at_epoch).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC#2's fsck wording with Section 5's vocabulary: replace 'unsigned/unverified count' with 'unsigned count' (presence-only, matching `state["authorship"]["unsigned"]`), and clarify that bad-signature detection is exclusively the merge-gate's job.
findings.finding: AC#2 uses 'unsigned/unverified count' for fsck, but Section 5 defines the reducer as recording ONLY signed/unsigned PRESENCE — never a cryptographic 'unverified' (bad-signature) verdict. 'Unverified' is ambiguous: it could mean unsigned OR bad-signature, but the reducer cannot distinguish them.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC#2 to: 'fsck surfaces a store-wide unsigned event count (events missing author_sig), consistent with the reducer's presence-only signed/unsigned tallies.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC1 test assertion for the 'bad-signature' verdict: a test submits an event with a present-but-cryptographically-invalid author_sig to verify-authorship and asserts it exits non-zero with a 'bad-signature' flag.
findings.finding: The merge-gate acceptance criterion tests only two of four failure verdicts (unsigned, unknown-author) but omits an explicit test assertion for the 'bad-signature' case (present author_sig that fails crypto verification) triggering a gate-fail (exit non-zero). This is a new user-facing flow with a whole failure class untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1; Section 4 (verify-authorship verdicts: verified / unsigned / unknown-author / bad-signature)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'FLAGS … an unsigned event, an unknown-author event, AND a bad-signature event (present sig that fails crypto verify) and exits non-zero; a test asserts all three failure cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) with a test that simulates a signing failure (e.g. invalid/unreadable key path when identity + signing_key are configured) and asserts the write succeeds, the event has no author_sig, and a warning is logged.
findings.finding: The write-path 'signing failure → log + write unsigned' behavior (Section 2: 'a signing failure logs + writes unsigned') is a new silent-failure mode with no acceptance criterion or test covering it. A caller cannot tell whether their event was signed or silently fell back to unsigned.
findings.location: Section 2 (Optional write-time event signing); Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A test configures a corrupt/unreadable signing_key, calls append_event, and asserts (a) the write does not raise, (b) the written event has no author_sig field, and (c) a WARNING-level log entry is emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `IdentityConfig` dataclass, add `identity: IdentityConfig` to `Config`, and register both `_SECTION_CLASSES['identity']` and `_SECTIONS['identity']` entries in `_config_schema.py` — the plan specifies the key values but omits these three required wiring steps.
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a `[identity]` config section with `identity.require_authenticated` and `identity.signing_key`, but does NOT call out the three mandatory wiring steps in `_config_schema.py`: (1) defining an `IdentityConfig` dataclass, (2) adding `identity: IdentityConfig` as a field on the `Config` dataclass, and (3) registering entries in both `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS`. Without all three, a `[tool.rebar.identity]` TOML block will be WARNED as an unknown section and the keys will not coerce or default correctly — the config will silently fall back to defaults and the gate will be permanently OFF.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@dataclass class IdentityConfig: require_authenticated: bool = False; signing_key: str | None = None` near the other config dataclasses; add `identity: IdentityConfig = field(default_factory=IdentityConfig)` to `Config`; add `'identity': IdentityConfig` to `_SECTION_CLASSES`; add `'identity': {'require_authenticated': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k), 'signing_key': lambda v, k: _as_str(v, k) or None}` to `_SECTIONS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `_config_schema.py`: `@dataclass class IdentityConfig` with both fields, register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES['identity']`, add its two coercers to `_SECTIONS['identity']`, and add `identity: IdentityConfig = field(default_factory=IdentityConfig)` to `Config`.
findings.finding: The new `[identity]` config section requires a new `IdentityConfig` dataclass AND registration in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, and the `Config` dataclass — four distinct additions to `_config_schema.py`. The plan (Section 1) only describes the two leaf keys (`require_authenticated`, `signing_key`) without naming the full schema-wiring machinery. Without the `_SECTIONS` coercer entry and `_SECTION_CLASSES` registration, a `[identity]` block in `rebar.toml` will trigger the unknown-section warning (or hard-error under `strict=True`) and the values will never be parsed.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the `VerifyConfig`/`verify` pattern exactly: (1) define `@dataclass class IdentityConfig: require_authenticated: bool = False; signing_key: str | None = None`; (2) add `'identity': IdentityConfig` to `_SECTION_CLASSES`; (3) add `'identity': {'require_authenticated': lambda v,k: _as_bool(v,k), 'signing_key': lambda v,k: _as_str(v,k) if v is not None else None}` to `_SECTIONS`; (4) add `identity: IdentityConfig = field(default_factory=IdentityConfig)` to `Config`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in Section 6 whether `epoch` in each ledger tuple is (a) taken from the event's own stored field (if any) or (b) recomputed via `epoch_for_position(author_id, event_position)` at compaction time, and add this as the epoch source for `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` as well.
findings.finding: Section 6 does not specify how the `epoch` field in each `authorship_ledger` tuple is determined during compaction — whether it is read from a stored field on the event envelope or recomputed via `epoch_for_position(author_id, event_position)` — leaving the executor to choose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The `epoch` field is determined by calling `epoch_for_position(author_id, event_position)` at compaction time (same call the merge-gate makes), so it is stable as long as the identity log is not rewritten.'

findings.finding: The `authorship_ledger` epoch field is ambiguous: Section 6 names `(event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch)` tuples but does not specify whether `epoch` is the epoch at write/sign time or the result of `epoch_for_position(author_id, event_position)` at compaction time — these can differ and the wrong choice silently breaks post-compaction verification.
findings.location: Section 6 — 'the SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` includes an `authorship_ledger` — the (event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch) tuples'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The `epoch` stored per entry is `epoch_for_position(author_id, position)` evaluated at fold/write time and preserved verbatim — the verifier must NOT recompute it from any post-compaction position.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact JSON schema for each `authorship_ledger` entry, including the serialization format of `author_sig` (e.g., `{payload_type, payload_b64, signatures: [{keyid, sig_b64}]}`) and the type/source of `epoch` (int from `epoch_for_position`), so compact.py and verify-authorship.py can independently implement compatible serialization and deserialization.
findings.finding: The `authorship_ledger` entry schema is referenced but never fully defined. The plan says each entry is `{event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch}` but does not specify: (a) the serialization format of `author_sig` (raw DSSE dict? base64-encoded bytes? a specific JSON shape?), (b) the type of `epoch` (int from epoch_for_position?), or (c) how `verify-authorship` reconstructs a `dsse.Envelope` from the stored `author_sig` to call `verify_authorship_at_epoch`.

Section 6 says the snapshot's authorship_ledger stores '(event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch) tuples' in compiled_state (a JSON document), but never specifies how the author_sig DSSE envelope is serialized for JSON storage. author_sig is a dsse.Envelope dataclass (payload_type, payload bytes, list[Signature]) that requires dsse.encode()/dsse.decode() for round-trip fidelity; storing it as a raw Python dict or omitting serialization would break the post-compaction verify-authorship call.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 6: 'Each ledger entry's author_sig is stored as a dsse.encode() JSON string and restored with dsse.decode() before being passed to verify_authorship_at_epoch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit graceful-degradation spec for legacy snapshots lacking `authorship_ledger` (e.g. 'treat as no-ledger: skip or warn'), and note whether a one-time migration/rebuild is required for existing compacted snapshots.
findings.finding: Old snapshots written before this change lack `authorship_ledger`. The plan states verify-authorship reads the ledger 'when the raw event files are gone,' but provides no rollback path or graceful-degradation spec for legacy snapshots missing the ledger.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 6: 'If compiled_state has no authorship_ledger key (legacy snapshot), verify-authorship emits a warning and treats all compacted events as unsigned (not bad-signature). Operators should run rebar rebuild-snapshot or rebar verify-authorship --all with raw events available to regenerate the ledger.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and test) the `verify-authorship` fallback when `authorship_ledger` is absent from a snapshot's compiled_state — e.g., treat all compacted events as `unsigned` and surface a warning, or skip with an explicit advisory.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `verify-authorship` behaves when it encounters a snapshot whose `compiled_state` lacks an `authorship_ledger` — i.e., snapshots compacted before this feature ships — leaving an operational gap where the command could crash, silently skip events, or produce misleading output on existing stores.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures) / Section 4 (verify-authorship reads the ledger when raw event files are gone)
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify_authorship.py`, when reading the snapshot: if `compiled_state.get('authorship_ledger')` is None, emit a warning ('snapshot predates authorship ledger; compacted events treated as unsigned') and count them as unsigned rather than crashing or silently skipping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how compact.py handles the case where the input to compaction is itself a snapshot: it must merge (carry forward) the prior snapshot's `authorship_ledger` entries in addition to scanning newly absorbed event files, so no ledger entries are lost across compaction cycles.
findings.finding: The authorship_ledger in compiled_state is built by compact.py scanning the event files it absorbs, but the plan does not specify what happens on a SECOND compaction of an already-compacted ticket (i.e., compact-over-snapshot). If compact.py only scans raw event files (not the prior snapshot's authorship_ledger), the earlier ledger entries are silently dropped, breaking post-compaction verify-authorship for those events.
findings.location: Section 6 — Snapshot / compact.py; Acceptance Criteria: 'Snapshot round-trip'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph to Section 6: 'When the source being absorbed is itself a snapshot (i.e., `compiled_state["authorship_ledger"]` already exists), compact.py merges those existing entries into the new ledger before scanning additional event files, deduplicating by `event_uuid`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` within `compact.py` as a second edit site that must also build `authorship_ledger` (or extract ledger-building into a shared helper called by both `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `compact.py` should build the `authorship_ledger` by scanning folded event files, but does NOT enumerate `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` as a SEPARATE edit target within `compact.py` that also needs the ledger-building logic. `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` is called by `fsck.py` (two call sites at lines 198-200 and 441-443) to repair snapshots — if it does not also build the `authorship_ledger`, post-fsck-repair snapshots will be missing the ledger and `verify-authorship --all` (reading from the snapshot ledger) will report all events as unsigned after an fsck repair.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the authorship_ledger scan into a helper `_build_authorship_ledger(event_files: list[str]) -> list[dict]` and call it from both `_compact_locked` (before retiring sources) and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` (over the full event list including retired files).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in §5 whether the `authorship` presence counts are accumulated FROM the snapshot's restored counts (cumulative over the full event history) or computed only over post-snapshot events; specify the initialization point explicitly so implementers don't reset to zero after a snapshot restore.
findings.finding: The plan specifies in §5 that the reducer records `state['authorship'] = {'signed': <count>, 'unsigned': <count>}` as a per-ticket presence summary, but does not say whether these counts are CUMULATIVE with the counts restored from a prior snapshot's `compiled_state`. After compaction, `process_snapshot` restores all `compiled_state` fields verbatim (including `authorship` with the pre-compaction counts). When the reducer then processes post-snapshot events, it needs to INCREMENT those restored counts — but the plan's wording ('records a per-ticket PRESENCE summary') could be read as replacing the field entirely each event, giving wrong (low) totals after compaction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to §6 an explicit algorithm for `verify-authorship` when a ticket has both a snapshot ledger and live post-snapshot event files: enumerate ledger entries for compacted events, then scan live (non-retired, non-snapshot) event files for the post-snapshot events, verify both sets, and union the verdicts deduplicated by event UUID.
findings.finding: Section 6 says `verify-authorship` reads the `authorship_ledger` from the snapshot when raw event files are gone, but does not specify the unification algorithm when a ticket has BOTH an `authorship_ledger` in its snapshot (covering compacted events) AND live post-snapshot event files with `author_sig` fields. The implementer must know: verify the ledger entries first, then verify live event files directly? Deduplicate by UUID? The plan is silent on this merge strategy.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures), AC — Snapshot round-trip

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement the `verify-authorship` fallback when a SNAPSHOT lacks `authorship_ledger` (e.g., treat pre-feature compacted events as advisory-unsigned rather than erroring), OR add a one-time migration that reconstructs ledgers from `*.retired` event files where they are still present.
findings.finding: Pre-existing compacted SNAPSHOTs lack `authorship_ledger`; the plan specifies that `verify-authorship` reads this ledger when raw event files are gone, but provides no migration or fallback for SNAPSHOTs already committed without the ledger — making post-feature verification of already-compacted tickets silently incomplete or broken.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures / compact.py) and Section 4 (verify-authorship merge-gate)
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify-authorship`, when a SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` has no `authorship_ledger`, scan the ticket directory for `*.retired` event files and attempt to reconstruct the ledger on the fly (same logic as the compaction scan). If retired files are also gone, emit a per-ticket warning 'authorship_ledger absent for pre-feature snapshot — coverage unknown' and count those source events as `unsigned` rather than failing the gate. Document this as the expected degraded state for history compacted before the feature was enabled.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the decision algorithm for `verify-authorship`'s source selection: define what 'raw event files are gone' means structurally, the fallback when `authorship_ledger` is absent (e.g., treat all compacted events as unsigned with a warning), and how to handle the hybrid case (SNAPSHOT ledger + post-snapshot raw events).
findings.finding: The merge-gate decision rule for sourcing authorship data when raw event files are gone (post-compaction) is unspecified. The plan says `verify-authorship` 'reads the ledger when the raw event files are gone' but provides no protocol for: (a) how it detects that raw files are gone, (b) what it does if it encounters a pre-existing SNAPSHOT lacking an `authorship_ledger`, or (c) how it handles a ticket with a mix of post-snapshot raw events and a snapshot-ledger.
findings.location: Section 6 — 'verify-authorship reads the ledger when the raw event files are gone'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in §2 that `author_sig` must be stored as the JSON string produced by `dsse.encode()` (not the `Envelope` dataclass), and correspondingly that `verify-authorship` calls `dsse.decode(event['author_sig'])` to reconstruct the `Envelope` before calling `verify_authorship_at_epoch`.
findings.finding: The plan says to store the DSSE-encoded envelope as `author_sig` on the event, but does not specify the wire format (JSON string from `dsse.encode()` vs. a Python dataclass object). `canonical_str` uses `json.dumps` which cannot serialize the `dsse.Envelope` dataclass directly and would raise a `TypeError` unless the implementer calls `dsse.encode()` first. This serialization step is implicit and could easily be missed.
findings.suggested_fix: In Section 2, change 'store the DSSE-encoded envelope as `author_sig`' to 'store the result of `dsse.encode(PAYLOAD_TYPE, payload, signatures)` as `author_sig` (a JSON string) on the event envelope; `verify-authorship` reconstructs the Envelope via `dsse.decode(event["author_sig"])`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `author_sig` is stored as the JSON string produced by `dsse.encode()` (not the raw `dsse.Envelope` object), and that `verify_authorship.py` reads it back with `dsse.decode()` before passing to `verify_authorship_at_epoch` — mirroring the `_encode_signature` / `dsse.decode` pattern in `identity.py`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'store the DSSE-encoded envelope as author_sig on the event envelope' but does not name the serialization call (dsse.encode()) needed before inserting into the event dict. A raw dsse.Envelope dataclass is not JSON-serializable; canonical_str(event) will raise a TypeError at write time if the implementer stores the Python object directly rather than its JSON string form. The existing precedent (_encode_signature() in identity.py) uses dsse.encode(), but the plan omits this detail.
findings.suggested_fix: In Section 2, replace 'store the DSSE-encoded envelope as author_sig' with 'store dsse.encode(envelope) (a JSON string) as author_sig'. In Section 4, specify that verify_authorship.py reads the stored string and calls dsse.decode(author_sig) before passing the resulting dsse.Envelope to verify_authorship_at_epoch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior of `verify-authorship` when a snapshot's `compiled_state` has no `authorship_ledger` (e.g., treat missing ledger as 'no signed events from this snapshot' and emit a warning, or document that pre-existing snapshots must be re-compacted after upgrading).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `verify-authorship` behaves when it encounters an existing snapshot that pre-dates this change (i.e., no `authorship_ledger` key present). This is a backward-compatibility non-happy-path: an operator running `--all` on a repo with older snapshots would get either an error or a silent false-pass (all events appear unsigned/unknown).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact set of event types (or the exemption rule) that `verify-authorship` skips at the merge-gate, consistent with the write-gate exemption list in Section 3, and co-locate this rule with the merge-gate processing protocol in Section 4.
findings.finding: Section 4 specifies verifying 'each mutating event' but never defines which event types are mutating vs. non-mutating/exempt, even though the set is load-bearing for the gate's scope. The write-gate (Section 3) references 'Gate- and lifecycle-exempt types — session_log/code_review/identity' but this exemption list is not explicitly carried over to the merge-gate verification scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 4: 'Exempt types (same as the write-gate): session_log, code_review, identity, SNAPSHOT — these are skipped by verify-authorship and never flagged as unsigned.'

findings.finding: Section 4 says the merge-gate must 'verify EACH mutating event' but never defines what event types are 'mutating' for this gate's purposes. Section 3 exempts session_log/code_review/identity from the write-gate, but Section 4 does not state whether these same types are exempt from the merge-gate scan, leaving the scope of --all undefined.
findings.location: Section 4 (merge-gate scope) and Section 3 (write-gate exempt types)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 4: 'The same gate-exempt types as the write-gate (session_log, code_review, identity) are skipped by the merge-gate scan; only mutating ticket-state events require a verified author_sig.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` to `file_impact` and explicitly specify that it must also scan folded/retired event files to populate `compiled_state['authorship_ledger']`, mirroring the `_compact_locked` scan described in Section 6.
findings.finding: `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` (the fsck crash-recovery path in compact.py) is not listed in `file_impact` and not mentioned in the plan's Section 6, meaning fsck-rebuilt SNAPSHOTs after the feature is deployed will lack `authorship_ledger` even for tickets whose raw/retired event files are still present — producing a ledger-absent snapshot from a full-history rebuild.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures) / file_impact list / compact.py:rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the authorship-ledger-building scan into a shared helper (e.g., `_build_authorship_ledger(event_files)`) and call it from both `_compact_locked` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` when composing the SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `identity.signing_key` belongs in `rebar.toml` (a tracked config file) rather than following the established pattern from `signing.py` (env var `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY`-equivalent or a gitignored per-machine config), and add a note about gitignoring if the toml path is retained.
findings.finding: The plan places `identity.signing_key` — a path to a private SSH private key — in `rebar.toml` (a tracked config file), which conflicts with the established in-codebase security pattern in `signing.py` where secrets are kept out of config files (env var `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` or a gitignored `.signing-key` file). No justification is given for diverging from this pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the signing.py precedent: use an `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var (or a gitignored per-machine override file) rather than a key in the committed rebar.toml. If the toml path is intentional (so it can point to ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 which is not committed), document that the key itself is never in the toml — only a filesystem path — and add a gitignore caveat.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in Section 6 that `authorship_ledger` is NOT added to `_snapshot_strip_keys` and that the strip set is intentionally exhaustive (only `updated_at` and optionally `signature`), so the survival invariant is a concrete spec constraint rather than an implicit assumption.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `authorship_ledger` 'must survive `_snapshot_strip_keys`' without specifying the mechanism — the current strip set is `{updated_at}` (plus optionally `signature`), and there is no defined exclusion that guarantees a newly-introduced `authorship_ledger` key won't be stripped by a future strip-key extension. This is an unverified capability assertion about a non-existent invariant.
findings.location: Section 6: 'It must survive _snapshot_strip_keys and be rebuilt identically by rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The strip set in `_snapshot_strip_keys` is intentionally exhaustive — `authorship_ledger` is NOT in it, so it flows through into the persisted SNAPSHOT compiled_state unchanged. A test should assert the key is present after compaction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit protocol rule for how `authorship_ledger` survives `_snapshot_strip_keys` (e.g., 'add a comment in `_snapshot_strip_keys` marking `authorship_ledger` as never-strip, and add a test asserting it is absent from the strip set').
findings.finding: The plan states the `authorship_ledger` 'must survive `_snapshot_strip_keys`' but provides no protocol rule for how — it does not say to add `authorship_ledger` to a keep-list, to change `_snapshot_strip_keys`'s logic, or to store the ledger outside `compiled_state`. The current `_snapshot_strip_keys` strips `updated_at` and optionally `signature`; no mechanism ensures `authorship_ledger` is not stripped by a future addition to that set.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section 6: '`authorship_ledger` is NOT added to the strip set; add an inline comment in `_snapshot_strip_keys` asserting this key must never be stripped; a unit test verifies `authorship_ledger not in _snapshot_strip_keys(emit_legacy_mirror=False)`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address partial-compaction failure for the authorship_ledger: either specify that the snapshot write is atomic (temp-file + rename) so the ledger is never partially written, or document that `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` is the recovery path and specify when it must be run.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the partial-compaction failure scenario: if `compact.py` is interrupted after absorbing some event files but before writing the finalized snapshot, the `authorship_ledger` may be incomplete relative to the events already removed, leaving the store in a state where `verify-authorship` underreports signed events with no recovery path.
findings.location: Section 6 (compact.py composes SNAPSHOT compiled_state) / file_impact compact.py
findings.suggested_fix: Ensure compact.py writes the snapshot (including authorship_ledger) atomically via a temp file + rename, consistent with any existing atomicity pattern in the codebase, so an interrupted compaction leaves the old snapshot intact and the raw event files untouched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete specification for how `authorship_ledger` survives `_snapshot_strip_keys` — either name the key in the allowlist pattern or state that `_snapshot_strip_keys` must be updated to explicitly preserve `compiled_state["authorship_ledger"]`.
findings.finding: The plan states the authorship_ledger 'must survive `_snapshot_strip_keys`' but does not specify the mechanism — it is unclear whether `authorship_ledger` is in an explicit allowlist, uses a different key prefix, or requires a change to the strip logic. This leaves the implementer with an under-specified constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the line-number citation in Section 1 from '(line 459)' to '(line 460)' — that is the actual location of `require_ticket_for_commit`'s `_as_bool` coercion in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan cross-references `_config_schema.py` line 459 as the location of the `require_ticket_for_commit` `_as_bool` pattern to mirror, but line 459 is `overlap_enabled`; `require_ticket_for_commit` is at line 460. This is a minor but empirically wrong citation that could cause the implementer to copy the wrong line.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(line 459)' to '(line 460)' in the plan text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/verify-authorship.txt` to `file_impact` (and create the file) so `rebar help verify-authorship` works, matching the parity of every other subcommand; alternatively, explicitly document that `verify-authorship.cli()` owns `--help` and the intercept in `main()` is placed before the `--help` short-circuit.
findings.finding: The plan does not list `src/rebar/_cli/help/verify-authorship.txt` in file_impact. While the `verify-commit-ticket` precedent (intercepted before `known_subcommands()`) means the command can work without a help file, the plan's AC states `rebar verify-authorship` should support `--help` (implied by 'owns its own --help'). Without a `help/verify-authorship.txt`, `rebar help verify-authorship` and `rebar verify-authorship --help` would emit 'Error: unknown subcommand' rather than the usage text, since `_emit_subcommand_help` calls `_help.subcommand_help(sub)` which looks up the help file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the file containing `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` to `file_impact` and specify that it must reconstruct `authorship_ledger` by re-scanning the full event log, mirroring the compact.py scan.
findings.finding: `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` is described as needing to rebuild the authorship_ledger identically after compaction, but the file containing this function is absent from `file_impact` — an executor following the impact list will miss this change.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures) and `## file_impact`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_commands/rebuild.py` (or wherever `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` lives) to `file_impact` with the note: 'reconstruct `compiled_state["authorship_ledger"]` by scanning all event files in position order and extracting `{event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch_for_position(author_id, pos)}` for each event that carries `author_id`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `show` command file to `file_impact` with a description of what authorship fields it must surface (e.g. `authorship: {signed: N, unsigned: M}` in the ticket's compiled-state output).
findings.finding: AC2 states '`show` surfaces the ticket's authorship status' but the file implementing `show` is absent from `file_impact`, creating a gap between the acceptance criterion and the described steps.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_commands/show.py` (or the relevant show-command file) to `file_impact` with: 'surface `state["authorship"]` counts in the ticket display output when present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `show` command file (e.g. `src/rebar/_commands/show.py`) to `file_impact` to cover the 'show surfaces authorship counts' deliverable described in Section 5 and tested by AC#2.
findings.finding: The `show` command file is absent from `file_impact` but Section 5 says '`show` surfaces the counts' and AC#2 requires 'show surfaces the ticket's authorship status' — a described deliverable with an AC has no listed file scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_commands/show.py` (or the actual show implementation file) to the `file_impact` list with a note: '(surface per-ticket signed/unsigned counts in ticket display)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` lives in `compact.py` or a separate file, and explicitly note its modification in `file_impact` (or annotate the compact.py entry to include it).
findings.finding: `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` is described in Section 6 as needing to rebuild the authorship_ledger identically from the full log, but this function is absent from `file_impact` — a contradiction between the described scope and the file change list.
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate the compact.py entry in file_impact: 'compact.py (authorship_ledger construction in both compact path AND rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the 'reducer=presence-only, merge-gate=crypto' invariant so it survives beyond this plan's prose.
findings.finding: The architectural rule that the reducer MUST NEVER perform cryptographic verification (presence-only counts) while the merge-gate holds EXCLUSIVE cryptographic authority is a load-bearing, non-obvious design decision that future contributors could violate — but the plan specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to make this durable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/authorship-enforcement.md` (or an entry in CLAUDE.md) recording: (1) reducer counts presence, never verifies signatures; (2) merge-gate holds exclusive crypto authority; (3) rationale (determinism, store always-readable). Reference it from the relevant source files as a comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art comparison in Section 6 justifying the `authorship_ledger`-in-snapshot design over established alternatives (e.g., exempting signing events from compaction as -SYNC.json is today, or a separate sidecar) and acknowledge the scalability tradeoff for high-event tickets.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel `authorship_ledger` list embedded in the SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` as the mechanism for preserving per-event signatures through compaction, with no consideration of established alternatives — exempting signing-relevant events from compaction (like -SYNC.json files today), a separate sidecar file, or re-signing the snapshot itself — and no justification for why the embedded-ledger approach is preferred.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures) and AC Snapshot round-trip

findings.finding: The authorship_ledger in the SNAPSHOT's compiled_state (Section 6) is not a reducer output — the reducer only records a presence-based 'authorship' summary (Section 5). Therefore compact.py must independently scan the folded event files and build the ledger (event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch tuples) BEFORE writing the SNAPSHOT. This extra compaction-time scan step is not reflected in file_impact (compact.py is absent from file_impact) and not specified in the plan, creating a mechanism gap: a post-compaction verify-authorship would silently find no ledger to verify against if the implementation only relies on reduce_ticket() output.
findings.suggested_fix: In Section 6, add a sentence: 'compact.py builds the authorship_ledger by scanning the folded event files (before retiring them) for author_id, author_sig, and calling epoch_for_position per event; this scan runs after reduce_ticket() and before the snapshot is written.' Add compact.py to file_impact.

findings.finding: Section 5 says the reducer records a per-ticket authorship summary (state['authorship'] = {'status': 'verified'|'unverified'|'unsigned', ...}) but specifies no aggregation rule for tickets with multiple events of mixed signing coverage. It is undefined whether the summary reflects the worst-case status, the last event's status, an event count, or something else.

The `file_impact` list places 'snapshot authorship_ledger' work in `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`, but the SNAPSHOT's `compiled_state` is actually composed and written in `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` (lines 178–213). The authorship_ledger must be injected into the snapshot dict before `canonical_str(snapshot_event)` is written — that logic lives in `compact.py`, which is absent from `file_impact`.
findings.location: file_impact section: 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py (never-reject + authorship status record + snapshot authorship_ledger)'
findings.suggested_fix: In file_impact, add: `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` (inject authorship_ledger into snapshot compiled_state at compact time). In Section 6, clarify: 'compact.py collects (event_uuid, author_id, author_sig, epoch) tuples from the events being folded and writes them as authorship_ledger into the SNAPSHOT's compiled_state; process_snapshot restores the ledger on replay.'

findings.finding: `compact.py` is named in Section 6 as requiring changes (adding `authorship_ledger` to `compiled_state` in snapshots, which flows through `_snapshot_strip_keys` and `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`) but is absent from `file_impact`.
findings.location: Section 6 (Snapshot carries folded signatures) / file_impact list
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` to the file_impact list with a note: '(authorship_ledger population in compiled_state for both _compact_locked and rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log snapshot-write paths)'.

findings.finding: The reducer (Section 5) uses 'verified' to mean 'both author_id and author_sig fields are present' (explicitly NOT a live crypto check), while the merge-gate (Section 4) uses 'verified' to mean 'cryptographically confirmed by verify_authorship_at_epoch'. The same term carries two distinct meanings across sections, which will mislead implementers and corrupt the 'authorship status' surfaced by show/fsck.
findings.location: Section 4 (merge-gate status vocabulary) vs Section 5 (reducer status record)
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the reducer's presence-based statuses to 'signed' (both fields present) / 'unsigned' (no author_sig) / 'missing-author' (no author_id), reserving 'verified'/'bad-signature' exclusively for the merge-gate's crypto verdicts.

findings.finding: The plan defines two non-aligned status enum vocabularies for authorship: the merge-gate (Section 4) uses a 4-value set {verified, unsigned, unknown-author, bad-signature} while the reducer compiled-state (Section 5) uses a 3-value set {verified, unverified, unsigned}. No mapping between them is specified, leaving an implementer to invent how bad-signature and unknown-author collapse into unverified (or some other value).
findings.location: Section 4 (merge-gate flag taxonomy) vs Section 5 (reducer state["authorship"] status enum)
findings.suggested_fix: Define a single canonical status enum (e.g., {verified, unsigned, unverified}) and map the merge-gate's richer diagnostic flags to it explicitly: unknown-author → unverified, bad-signature → unverified. The merge-gate output can still surface the richer diagnostic; the reducer records the canonical 3-value status.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
